Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered, which I know sounds bad given my grades. I'd usually dedicate chunks of time on weekends and maybe two evenings during the week, totaling around 18 hours. I relied heavily on reading and writing out notes by hand, then typing them up later. The routine worked for me even if it didn't always look organized from the outside.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. I have extracurricular commitments that sometimes ate into my study time, so I'd compensate by cramming more intensely closer to deadlines. Some weeks I'd be super consistent, others I'd barely touch anything until the pressure mounted.
Q3: Usually it was checking if there were new announcements or if grades had been posted. Sometimes I'd log in to download materials I needed for offline studying. Honestly, I wasn't logging in constantly—I'd batch my LMS visits rather than checking multiple times a day. Deadlines approaching would definitely prompt more frequent logins.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a daily habit for me—it felt more like a tool I used when necessary rather than something integrated into my routine. I'd remember to check it maybe a few times a week, but it wasn't automatic. My routine was more about the actual studying than the platform itself.
Q5: The reading materials and lecture notes were essential for me since I learn best through text. I'd download PDFs and work through them with a highlighter and notebook. The assignment submission portal was obviously necessary, but the actual learning resources in text form were what I valued most. Video content was useful too, though I often watched at 1.5x speed.
Q6: I mostly ignored the discussion forums—I only posted once the whole semester. They felt performative and time-consuming for minimal learning benefit. I also didn't engage much with peer interaction features beyond what was absolutely required. It's not that I'm antisocial, but I found those activities didn't match how I actually learn best.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly contributed to my understanding or my grade. I'm pretty pragmatic about this—if I couldn't see how something would help me perform better on assignments or exams, I'd deprioritize it. Clear connections to assessment criteria made me more willing to invest effort.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford given my stress levels and other commitments. Required activities had built-in accountability, so I'd make sure those got done even when I was exhausted. With optional stuff, I'd think 'that looks interesting' but then never actually get to it because something more urgent always came up.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download everything at the start of a module and work from printed notes or my tablet without internet. I used external textbooks from the library and supplementary YouTube videos that weren't part of the course. My actual study time far exceeded what the LMS could ever capture.
Q10: There were entire weekends where I'd study for eight or ten hours using downloaded materials and my handwritten notes, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd also review past assignments and practice problems I'd saved locally. Sometimes I'd be deep in concentration with course content spread across my desk, completely disconnected from the platform.
Q11: This was basically my standard approach. I'd log in, download what I needed, maybe skim the video descriptions, and then work offline for hours or even days. I found it less distracting to study without the temptation of checking other tabs or notifications. My internet access is good, but I chose to disconnect for focus.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal—my stress levels peaked and I felt genuinely bored with some of the content, which is a dangerous combination. My attendance dropped because I was overwhelmed trying to balance everything. There were weeks where just opening the LMS felt exhausting, even though I knew I needed to push through.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or stressed, I'd do the bare minimum on the LMS—quick logins, grab materials, leave. My extracurriculars and personal responsibilities sometimes meant I couldn't engage as deeply as I wanted. The platform became purely functional during those periods rather than a space for exploration or engagement.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing while my mind wandered somewhere else entirely. Sometimes I'd scroll through readings without actually processing anything because I was too anxious or tired. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than genuine learning.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because they felt low-value compared to assignments. I'd also avoid starting big assignments until closer to deadlines because the initial activation energy felt insurmountable when I was already stressed. I knew this wasn't ideal, but my resilience kicked in when it really mattered.
Q16: Deadlines were honestly my primary driver for LMS activity. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active—downloading materials, reviewing requirements, submitting work. Without that external pressure, I'd drift toward other priorities. My task completion rate reflects this pattern of deadline-driven bursts.
Q17: Feedback definitely motivated me when it was specific and constructive. Getting good scores on assignments reinforced that my offline study methods were working. If I received criticism, I'd go back and review materials more carefully. But I didn't constantly check for feedback—I'd look when I remembered or when grades were released.
Q18: The course structure gave me flexibility, which I needed but also maybe abused. Having materials available to download meant I could work on my own schedule, but it also meant I could disengage from the platform for long stretches. Weekly modules helped me pace myself somewhat, though I often fell behind and caught up in bursts.
Q19: The availability of text-based resources made engagement easier since that matches my learning style. What made it harder was when participation in discussions was encouraged but not required—I'd skip those entirely. Also, the volume of video content was sometimes overwhelming; I wished for more concise written summaries.
Q20: I think those students might be searching for something, maybe confused about requirements or trying to find specific information. They could also be anxious perfectionists who keep checking and rechecking without actually submitting. Or maybe they're dealing with stress that makes starting work feel impossible even when they're present on the platform.
Q21: This absolutely reflects my experience. I performed well because my learning happened offline—reading, writing, practicing with downloaded materials. The LMS was a delivery mechanism, not where my actual engagement occurred. Low platform activity doesn't mean low effort; it might mean efficient, focused work happening elsewhere.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access and submission timing pretty accurately. They capture when you're active on the platform and what you click on. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, offline study, emotional state, and whether someone actually understood what they were looking at. They measure presence, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm genuinely thinking about the material, connecting it to what I already know, and caring about understanding it properly. It's not about how many times I log in or how long I watch videos. Real engagement is mental investment—being curious, wrestling with difficult concepts, and wanting to master the content.
Q24: The LMS would never show my late-night study sessions with printed notes spread across my floor, or the anxiety I managed through mindfulness exercises before tackling difficult assignments. It wouldn't capture how I'd explain concepts to myself out loud while walking, or the satisfaction I felt when something finally clicked. My resilience and determination are invisible to data.
